narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, a lower-middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 33, an undergraduate GPA of 3.00, and planning to attend full-time—compared to typical students who rank much higher in school deciles, have higher average LSATs around 37 and GPAs around 3.21, and come from somewhat higher income backgrounds. The model predicted a 61.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar right away. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking at the bottom, as it stood out strongly compared to most students who perform much better in later years. Your first-year decile at the bottom also contributed noticeably in the same direction, signaling early academic challenges that the model weighs heavily. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA played a helpful role, nudging the prediction toward passing since it's close to the typical student's level. Your family income bracket similarly helped a bit by aligning with patterns of students who still succeed despite not being in the highest earners. Your LSAT score and full-time enrollment status provided smaller boosts toward passing, as they're in line with common successful profiles. Overall, your lower school deciles were the most influential hurdles, far outweighing these strengths for this particular prediction. While this is disappointing, especially given the barriers many Black women face in competitive fields like law, it's not the end of your journey—consider retaking courses or the LSAT to boost your decile rankings and scores, seek targeted tutoring or mentorship programs for underrepresented students, and reapply after building a stronger academic record. You've already shown resilience by getting this far, and with focused improvements on those key areas, you can shift the odds in your favor next time. We're rooting for you.
